categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From Umayyad province to independent caliphate — the Córdoba dynasty

entries.label: Tariq ibn Ziyad — the conquest of Hispania
entries.hint: Tariq ibn Ziyad crosses from Africa with 7,000 troops in April 711 — Gibraltar (Jabal Tariq, 'mountain of Tariq') is named after him; within 7 years nearly the entire peninsula is under Muslim control; only small Christian communities survive in the Asturian mountains.
entries.note: Chronicle of 754 (written in al-Andalus by a Christian, 754 CE): the most contemporary account — primary source. The earliest al-Andalus coin (716 CE, dinar): Archaeological Museum of Madrid — bears 'al-Andalus' in Arabic and 'Span' in Latin on the same coin. Collins (1989), The Arab Conquest of Spain: the standard modern study.

entries.label: Abd al-Rahman I — founding the independent emirate
entries.hint: The last surviving Umayyad prince flees the Abbasid massacre in Damascus and reaches al-Andalus — defeats the Abbasid governor and establishes an independent emirate; he begins the Great Mosque of Córdoba in 785; his dynasty will rule for 275 years.
entries.note: Abd al-Rahman I's flight and arrival: Ibn al-Athir (12th c.) and al-Maqqari (17th c.) drawing on earlier sources. Great Mosque of Córdoba construction begun 785: contemporary building accounts lost but the mosque itself is the primary source; first expansion documented. Kennedy (1996), Muslim Spain and Portugal: the standard modern survey.

entries.label: Abd al-Rahman III — Caliph of the West
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III declares himself Caliph on 16 January 929 — the first Caliph of al-Andalus, challenging both the Abbasids and Fatimids; he builds Madinat al-Zahra (begun 936) as his palace city; his court exchanges embassies with the Byzantine Emperor, Otto I and the Holy Roman Empire.
entries.note: Proclamation of the Caliphate 929: contemporary chronicle by ʿĪsā al-Rāzī (annals 971–975, the court chronicler). Madinat al-Zahra (begun 936): archaeological site west of Córdoba (extant, UNESCO). The Byzantine embassy: documented in Constantine Porphyrogenitus's De Ceremoniis (Constantinople) and in Arabic sources. Wasserstein (1985), The Caliphate in the West: the standard study.

entries.label: Al-Hakam II — the scholar-caliph
entries.hint: Al-Hakam II builds the Córdoba caliphate's greatest library — 400,000 volumes (the largest in Europe by far); he personally annotates manuscripts and corresponds with scholars across the Islamic world; he brings the Greek Dioscorides manuscript to Córdoba and funds its Arabic translation.
entries.note: The library of 400,000 volumes: al-Maqqari (drawing on contemporary sources). The Dioscorides translation: documented — a Byzantine monk Nicolas arrived with the manuscript (documented in Arabic sources and cross-referenced in Archnet database). Al-Hakam's personal annotations on manuscripts: reported in sources. Scales (1994), The Fall of the Caliphate of Córdoba: the standard study of the caliphate's decline.

entries.label: Almanzor (al-Mansur) — the regent who eclipsed the caliph
entries.hint: Ibn Abi Amir al-Mansur (Almanzor) rules as regent for the child caliph Hisham II — the real power for 24 years; he leads 57 documented military campaigns against the Christian kingdoms, sacks Santiago de Compostela (997), carries off its bells; his death triggers the caliphate's collapse.
entries.note: Almanzor's campaigns: Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib (13th c. but drawing on contemporary sources). The sack of Santiago 997: documented in both Arabic and Christian sources. His 57 campaigns: listed in Arabic sources. Kennedy (1996) pp.107–120. The irony: the Christian kingdom's most sacred site destroyed by a Muslim regent — the bells carried to Córdoba as lamp holders.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: The conquest, the Battle of Tours, and the northern frontier

entries.label: Umayyad advance into France — Battle of Tours/Poitiers
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman al-Ghafiqi leads an Umayyad army deep into Francia — Charles Martel defeats him at Tours/Poitiers in October 732; the battle's importance has been debated (was it decisive?) but it marks the northernmost limit of Muslim advance in Western Europe.
entries.note: Chronicle of 754 (primary, by a Christian in al-Andalus): calls it the 'Battle of the Court of Martyrs'. Fredegar's Chronicle continuation: Frankish perspective. Charles Martel's role: documented. The battle's significance debate: Riche (1993) vs Collins (1989) — both use the same thin primary sources. The Muslim withdrawal: subsequent Arabic sources attribute it to raiding rather than permanent conquest.

entries.label: Roncesvalles — the ambush that created the Song of Roland
entries.hint: Charlemagne's retreating army is ambushed in the Roncesvalles Pass — the historical attackers are Basques, not Muslims, but the Chanson de Roland (c.1100) transforms them into a Muslim army; the gap between the historical event and its literary transformation is a primary example of medieval mythmaking.
entries.note: Einhard, Vita Karoli Magni (c.830): primary source — explicitly says Basques, names Roland as one of the dead. The Annales Regni Francorum: contemporary but vague. The Chanson de Roland (c.1100): BnF manuscript (Oxford copy). The transformation from Basques to Muslims: the clearest example of how the Reconquista narrative reshaped historical memory. Short (1990), La Chanson de Roland.

entries.label: The Martyrs of Córdoba — Christian provocation under Muslim rule
entries.hint: A group of Christians in Córdoba deliberately seek martyrdom by publicly insulting Islam — 48 are executed between 850 and 859; Eulogius of Córdoba documents them; the episode reveals the tensions within the Mozarab community between accommodation and resistance to Muslim rule.
entries.note: Eulogius of Córdoba, Memoriale Sanctorum (851–858) and Documentum Martyriale (c.857): primary source — Eulogius was himself eventually executed (859). The Arabic-language response: the Qadi Ibn Ziyad's counter-arguments (preserved in Christian sources). Coope (1995), The Martyrs of Córdoba: the standard modern study. The episode documents both Christian-Muslim tension and the substantial Mozarab community.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Islamic governance, the dhimma system and Umayyad administration

entries.label: The dhimma — legal status of Christians and Jews under Islam
entries.hint: The Muslim conquest establishes the dhimma system — Christians and Jews are 'People of the Book', protected but subject to the jizya tax; the Pact of Tudmir (713) is the primary documented example, giving the Christian lord Theodemir (Tudmir) his lands in exchange for submission.
entries.note: Pact of Tudmir (713 CE): Arabic text preserved in al-Dabbi (13th c.) — translated Constable (1997), Medieval Iberia: Readings from Christian, Muslim and Jewish Sources. This is the best-documented early Muslim-Christian accommodation agreement in al-Andalus. Collins (1989) pp.38–42. The dhimma's practical application: documented in later legal sources and administrative records.

entries.label: Caliphate of Córdoba — administrative structure
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III creates a sophisticated caliphal administration — diwan (ministry) system, tax collection through amilship, royal tiraz (textile workshops), mint producing the first al-Andalus gold dinars; the annals of ʿĪsā al-Rāzī (971–975) document the daily operations of this court.
entries.note: ʿĪsā al-Rāzī's court annals (971–975): the extraordinary source that records daily court events from June 971 to July 975 — analysed in Manzano Moreno (2023), The Court of the Caliphate of al-Andalus (Edinburgh): the standard study using this source. The first al-Andalus gold dinars (929): documented — marking Abd al-Rahman III's caliphate claim. The Madinat al-Zahra archaeology: extant site.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Islam, Mozarab Christianity, Judaism and the question of convivencia

entries.label: Great Mosque of Córdoba — the architectural primary source
entries.hint: The Great Mosque of Córdoba (begun 785) is expanded by every major ruler — Abd al-Rahman I, II, III, al-Hakam II and Almanzor each add sections; the building itself is a chronological primary source documenting caliphal ambition and wealth across 200 years.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1984). Construction phases: documented in Arabic sources and confirmed archaeologically. The horseshoe arches reusing Visigothic and Roman columns: physical evidence of cultural layering. Al-Hakam II's expansion (961–976): the most ornate — the mihrab mosaics made by Byzantine craftsmen (documented in Arabic sources). Dodds (1992), Architecture and Ideology in Early Medieval Spain: the standard art-historical study.

entries.label: Hasdai ibn Shaprut — the Jewish vizier of Córdoba
entries.hint: Hasdai ibn Shaprut serves as Abd al-Rahman III's physician, diplomat and de facto foreign minister — he corresponds with the Byzantine Emperor and the Khazar King in Hebrew; his career is the clearest documented example of Jewish political participation at the summit of an Islamic state.
entries.note: Hasdai's letter to the Khazar King Joseph (c.960): Hebrew text preserved in multiple manuscripts; published Kobler (1978), Letters of Jews Through the Ages. His diplomatic role: documented in Arabic sources (al-Maqqari) and confirmed by Byzantine records. The Byzantine-Córdoba correspondence he handled: cross-referenced in Byzantine sources. Mann (1920), The Jews in Egypt and Palestine: the older standard study.

entries.label: Mozarab community — Christians under Muslim rule
entries.hint: The Mozarab Christians continue their faith under Muslim rule — they pay the jizya, maintain churches, copy manuscripts and produce scholars like Eulogius; the term 'Mozarab' (from Arabic mustarib, 'one who adopts Arab ways') documents the cultural integration that shaped a distinctive Iberian Christianity.
entries.note: Eulogius of Córdoba (died 859): primary source for Mozarab life. The Mozarabic manuscripts (9th–10th c.): Real Academia de la Historia. The Beatus manuscripts (commentaries on the Apocalypse, made in Christian monasteries under Muslim rule): UNESCO Memory of the World. Simonet (1897), Historia de los Mozárabes de España: the older comprehensive study.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Andalusian architecture, Beatus manuscripts and court culture

entries.label: Madinat al-Zahra — the palace city of the caliphs
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III builds Madinat al-Zahra as a new capital city — 1.5 sq km, housing the caliphal court, government offices, gardens and mosque; partially destroyed in civil war (1010) and abandoned; ongoing excavations since 1910 make it the richest archaeological site of Islamic Spain.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 2018). Construction begun 936–940: documented in Arabic sources (al-Maqqari). The reception halls: excavated and partially restored. Vallejo Triano (2010), La ciudad califal de Madinat al-Zahra: the standard archaeological study. The deliberate siting: to challenge the Fatimid and Abbasid caliphates — political ideology made architecture.

entries.label: Beatus of Liébana manuscripts — Christian art under Islam
entries.loc: Monasteries in Christian north; copied in Mozarab south
entries.hint: The Beatus of Liébana's Commentary on the Apocalypse (c.776) is illuminated in scores of manuscripts by Mozarab artists — the most distinctive Christian art of the period; the Girona Beatus (975) is the best preserved; the vivid colours and flat figures document a Christian artistic tradition shaped by its Islamic environment.
entries.note: UNESCO Memory of the World (Beatus manuscripts, 2015). The Morgan Beatus (c.940–945): Pierpont Morgan Library, New York (MS M.644). The Girona Beatus (975): Cathedral Archive of Girona. Williams (1994–2003), The Illustrated Beatus: A Corpus (5 vols): the complete catalogue. The artistic style's Islamic influences: documented in Williams's analysis.

entries.label: Ziryab — the musician who transformed Andalusian culture
entries.hint: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn Nafi (Ziryab) arrives in Córdoba from Baghdad in 822 — he is the greatest musician of the medieval Mediterranean, establishes the first music conservatory in Europe, adds a fifth string to the lute, codifies Andalusian music theory, and introduces new fashions, cuisine and etiquette to the Córdoba court.
entries.note: Ziryab's arrival and role: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (10th-c. court chronicle); al-Maqqari, Nafh al-Tib (drawing on Ibn Hayyan). The fifth string added to the oud: documented in Arabic musical theory sources. Shiloah (1995), Music in the World of Islam: the standard study. Ziryab's influence on Andalusian (flamenco-precursor) music: documented in the maqamat tradition.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Córdoba as the intellectual capital of Western Europe

entries.label: Al-Zahrawi (Abulcasis) — the father of surgery
entries.hint: Abu al-Qasim al-Zahrawi (Abulcasis) is al-Hakam II's court physician — his Kitab al-Tasrif (The Method of Medicine, 30 volumes) contains the first illustrated surgical treatise; 200 surgical instruments are described and depicted; it remained the standard European surgical text for 500 years.
entries.note: Kitab al-Tasrif (c.1000 CE): multiple Arabic manuscripts; Vol.30 (the surgical treatise) translated into Latin by Gerard of Cremona c.1150. Earliest manuscripts: Biblioteca Medicea Laurenziana, Florence. Hamarneh & Sonnedecker (1963), A Pharmaceutical View of Abulcasis al-Zahrawi: the standard English study. The Latin translation's influence: documented in European medical manuscript transmission.

entries.label: Ibn Hayyan — the greatest Andalusian historian
entries.hint: Ibn Hayyan is the greatest historian of al-Andalus — his Muqtabis (an annalistic history of the Umayyad period) and Matin (a contemporary chronicle) are the primary narrative sources for the caliphate at its height; large portions survive only in later quotations.
entries.note: Muqtabis: partially surviving in multiple volumes. Vol. II (reign of Abd al-Rahman II): Madrid Real Academia de la Historia. Vol. V (reign of Abd al-Rahman III): published by García Gómez (1967). Matin: largely lost; fragments quoted in later authors. Kennedy (1996) uses Ibn Hayyan throughout. The partial survival: a result of the sack of Córdoba's libraries (1009–1031).

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: The agricultural revolution and the wealth of al-Andalus

entries.label: Arab agricultural revolution — new crops transform Iberia
entries.hint: The Muslim conquest introduces new crops and irrigation technology to Iberia — cotton, sugar cane, rice, saffron, citrus fruits, artichokes, aubergines and hard wheat (durum) are brought from the Islamic world; the Valencian huerta (irrigated garden) system is established.
entries.note: The Arabic agricultural manuals: Ibn Bassal's Libro de Agricultura (11th c.) — Real Biblioteca del Monasterio de El Escorial; Abu'l-Khayr al-Ishbili's Kitab al-Filaha (12th c.). Watson (1983), Agricultural Innovation in the Early Islamic World: the standard study of the 'Arab agricultural revolution'. The Valencian irrigation system: still operating today — documented from Arabic sources and continued in use.

entries.label: Córdoba at its peak — the largest city in Western Europe
entries.hint: At its 10th-century peak, Córdoba has a population of 100,000–300,000 (estimates vary widely) — the largest city in Western Europe by far; it has 3,000 mosques, 300 public baths, paved streets with lighting; the contrast with contemporary London or Paris (populations in the low thousands) is dramatic.
entries.note: Population estimates: al-Maqqari's figures are almost certainly exaggerated (500,000); modern estimates range 100,000–300,000 — documented in Kennedy (1996) pp.165–167. The 300 public baths: documented in Arabic sources. The comparison with northern Europe: Whitelock (1952) on Anglo-Saxon London. The uncertainty must be flagged: population figures in medieval sources are notoriously unreliable.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Diplomacy, translation and the transmission of knowledge

entries.label: Córdoba and Byzantium — caliphal diplomacy
entries.hint: Abd al-Rahman III exchanges embassies with the Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII — the Byzantine mission brings a Greek manuscript of Dioscorides; a Byzantine monk Nicolas is sent to translate it; the diplomatic exchange documents how the Córdoba caliphate operated on equal terms with Constantinople.
entries.note: The Byzantine embassy: documented in Constantine Porphyrogenitus's De Ceremoniis (Constantinople) and cross-referenced in Arabic sources including ʿĪsā al-Rāzī's court annals. The Dioscorides manuscript: identified as the Vienna Dioscorides or a copy. The monk Nicolas's arrival: documented in Arabic sources. Archnet documentation confirms the Byzantine mosaicists at the Great Mosque.

entries.label: The 716 coin — al-Andalus names itself
entries.hint: A dinar struck in 716 — just 5 years after the conquest — bears 'al-Andalus' in Arabic on one side and 'Span' in Latin on the other; the earliest attestation of the Arabic name for the territory; the bilingual coin documents the new political entity negotiating its identity in two scripts simultaneously.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: The 716 dinar: Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid. The 'Span' inscription: the earliest dated use of this Latin form. Published in Miles (1950), The Coinage of the Umayyads of Spain. Cited in Wikipedia article on Al-Andalus as 'the earliest attestation of this Arab name'. The bilingual coin is the clearest single object documenting the conquest and naming.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Al-Andalus in its Mediterranean and global context

entries.label: The Caliphate collapses — the taifa period begins
entries.hint: After Almanzor's death (1002) the caliphate fractures in civil war — the fitna (civil strife) of 1009–1031 sees Berber troops sack Madinat al-Zahra, al-Hakam II's library dispersed, and the caliphate dissolve into 23 taifa (faction) kingdoms; the greatest Islamic polity in the West disintegrates.
entries.note: The fitna documented: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (fragments quoted in later sources). The sack of Madinat al-Zahra c.1009–1010: documented in Arabic chronicles. The dissolution of the caliphate 1031: formal deposition of Hisham III — documented. Scales (1994), The Fall of the Caliphate of Córdoba: the standard study. The dispersal of al-Hakam II's library: documented loss — a cultural catastrophe.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including the convivencia debate

entries.label: Subh — the Basque concubine who ruled al-Andalus
entries.hint: Subh, a Basque concubine of Caliph Hisham II, effectively co-rules al-Andalus with Almanzor — she controls the caliph's seal, manages correspondence and is documented wielding executive power; her eventual break with Almanzor in 996 triggers a political crisis documented in court sources.
entries.note: Subh's role: Ibn Hayyan, Muqtabis (fragments). Her management of the caliphal seal: documented in contemporary sources. Her correspondence: some letters preserved. Rubiera Mata (1992), La mujer en al-Andalus: the standard study of women in Andalusian history. The break with Almanzor 996: documented. Subh is the best-documented woman in 10th-century Iberia, Muslim or Christian.

entries.label: The convivencia debate — coexistence or oppression?
entries.hint: The degree of Jewish-Christian-Muslim coexistence in al-Andalus is the most contested question in Iberian medieval historiography — Américo Castro (1954) argued for a Golden Age of tolerance; Brian Catlos (2014) argues it was pragmatic coexistence under hierarchy; the dhimma documents legal inequality alongside cultural flowering.
entries.note: Américo Castro, España en su Historia (1948, translated 1954): the foundational 'convivencia' argument. Catlos (2014), Infidel Kings and Unholy Warriors: the modern critical reassessment. The dhimma system documentation: Pact of Tudmir (713). Hasdai ibn Shaprut's career: evidence for Jewish participation at the summit of power. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT entry not for a historical event but for a historiographic framework — the debate itself is a primary fact about how Spain constructs its medieval past.

events.l: Ibn Hayyan — historian of the caliphate
Córdoba — largest city in Western Europe
